channel.title: After the Grind
channel.description: Recent content on After the Grind

item.title: What Ian Beacraft Means for Universities
item.description: Ian Beacraft’s insights force universities, especially business schools, to confront a fundamental question: What is the purpose of education in an era where artificial intelligence reshapes not just tasks but the very architecture of work? Beacraft argues that AI is not a tool but a platform that rewrites economic and organizational rules. This distinction is critical. If universities continue to teach business as if AI is merely a productivity enhancer, they risk preparing students for a world that no longer exists. The stakes are clear: students will enter workplaces where efficiency is ubiquitous, and survival depends on understanding systemic shifts, not incremental optimization. Business schools must pivot from technical training to cultivating systems thinking, cultural intelligence, and interdisciplinary agility.

item.title: The Migration Has Changed
item.description: For a century, the path to professional success followed a single geographic rule. You left home. You moved to the hotspot. Musicians packed their instruments and headed to Nashville or New York. Coders booked flights to Silicon Valley. Finance professionals chased the tape on Wall Street. Filmmakers followed the lights to Hollywood. Fashion designers packed their sketches for Paris and Milan. The pattern was brutal but clear. If you stayed put, you starved. The network lived in those cities. The capital lived there. The gatekeepers lived there. You had to be there to be seen.

item.title: The Involuntary Pilot Program
item.description:
The GSA just lost 40% of its workforce and is now automating a million work hours to compensate. This isn’t a cautionary tale. It’s a preview of the substitution-versus-augmentation split playing out everywhere, and of what the 4I model actually protects against.

Read that again slowly. Forty percent of the people. Gone. Now AI fills the gap.

Nobody at the GSA called this a pilot program. But that is exactly what it is. A large institution, stripped of human capacity by circumstance rather than choice, is now discovering in real time which of its functions an AI system can absorb. The results will be instructive for every organization watching from the sidelines.

item.title: The Law That Is Changing the Education of Humanity
item.description:
The math of the solar industry is not an anomaly. It is a template.

In 1977, the cost of solar power was approximately six dollars per watt. Today, that cost has plummeted to well below twenty cents per watt. This collapse did not happen through random chance or minor incrementalism. It happened because of Swanson’s Law: for every doubling of cumulative solar panel production, the cost of solar energy drops by roughly twenty percent. A predictable, compounding decay of cost driven by scale and technological refinement.

item.title: The Model That Scared Its Creator
item.description: Anthropic built a model too dangerous to release, and that fact alone tells you everything about where we are.

item.title: You Sound Crazy (And How to Stop)
item.description: Being deep in AI is a communication problem as much as a knowledge problem, and the fix isn’t dumbing it down.

item.title: If Your Faculty Aren't Burning Tokens, You Have a Problem. So Do Your Students.
item.description: Jensen Huang says if a $500,000 engineer isn’t consuming $250,000 in tokens, something is broken. He’s right. And higher education should be very uncomfortable with what that implies.

item.title: The Graph That Goes Down and to the Right
item.description: Germany shut down its last nuclear plant in 2025. The graph of their nuclear output is a straight line from 170 terawatt-hours down to zero. Every time you see a graph that goes down and to the right, it is failure. This one is no different.

item.title: The Fear Index Is Up. The Strategy Is Wrong.
item.description: A new Quinnipiac poll shows AI anxiety at record highs, and a Tufts study maps 9.3 million vulnerable jobs. But the most useful data this week came from a survey of college interns who figured out something most adults haven’t: the right response to automation isn’t fear. It’s a pivot.

item.title: The Vanishing First Rung
item.description: Tech companies cut 59,000 jobs in three months while posting record revenues. BlackRock’s CEO worries about the class of 2026. The entry-level job ladder is disappearing. Here’s what that actually means.

item.title: Every Professor Needs an Agent
item.description: Universities are debating AI policies. Meanwhile, every faculty member should already have a local AI agent running on their own hardware with a private model. Here’s why.

item.title: The Fieri Model: What Guy Fieri's New Show Reveals About the Future of Credentials
item.description: Two franchise models. One tells you exactly what to cook. The other validates that you can cook. The second one is the future of professional credentials.

item.title: The Gap Between Could and Does
item.description: Anthropic’s new labor market report reveals AI could automate far more jobs than it actually does. That gap is where human value lives.

item.title: Three Futures, One Question
item.description: A financial analyst, an investment strategist, and a tech optimist each wrote a letter from the future. They agree on almost everything except the part that matters most: what happens to the people.

item.title: Agents and the Work-to-Signal Ratio
item.description:
Agents and the Work-to-Signal Ratio

Think about the last time you researched something for work. Not the moment you understood it. The hours before that moment. The Googling. The opening of fourteen tabs. The copying of relevant paragraphs into a document. The reformatting. The rewriting so it sounded like you and not like a Wikipedia article. The making of a chart because someone on the team “needs to see the visual.” The uploading, the linking, the publishing.

item.title: How Agents Break the Procrastination Loop
item.description: Procrastination isn’t laziness. It’s task aversion, driven by the friction between knowing what you want and actually starting. Agentic AI collapses that friction, and what’s left is the work you actually want to do.

item.title: The Invisible Admissions Office
item.description: Students are asking ChatGPT where to go to college. If your institution isn’t showing up in those answers, you might as well not exist.

item.title: The Resume Is Dead
item.description: When AI can do the credentialed work, credentials stop being the signal. What replaces them? Judgment, taste, and the ability to sit with ambiguity.

item.title: The Last Moment That Matters
item.description: Tech layoffs are accelerating, Microsoft says full automation is 18 months away, and a former Google ethicist warns of a jobs collapse by 2027. The question isn’t whether AI will change everything. It’s whether we’ll prepare for what comes after.

item.title: The AI-Washing Problem: When Companies Blame the Robot
item.description: Tech layoffs keep climbing and CEOs keep pointing at AI. But a growing body of evidence suggests the robot is taking the blame for decisions that have nothing to do with automation.

item.title: The Solow Paradox Returns: AI Is Everywhere Except Where It Counts
item.description: AI dominates headlines but barely registers in economic data. Meanwhile, millions of workers lack the adaptive capacity to navigate what’s coming. The gap between the noise and the reality tells us something important about human value.

item.title: Eighteen Months to What, Exactly?
item.description: Microsoft’s AI chief says white-collar work will be automated in 18 months. Economists see jobless growth. Workers are already pivoting. The real question isn’t whether the grind disappears. It’s what replaces it.

item.title: The Grind Is Ending Faster Than You Think
item.description: Microsoft’s AI chief says 18 months. Capgemini is betting €700 million. UC enrollment is shifting. The signals are converging.

item.title: About
item.description:
Andrew Perkins is Chair of the Department of Marketing and International Business at Washington State University’s Carson College of Business.

His research spans consumer psychology, implicit cognition, and the forces that shape human judgment beneath conscious awareness. Over 25 years of published work, he has studied how surface signals shape perception (from wine labels to celebrity voice-overs), how implicit attitudes shift without our permission, how bodies become sites of social judgment, and how power, nostalgia, and awe shape the choices we make. His most recent work examines when moral outrage leads to public condemnation, and how identity and group membership shape that response.

item.title: After the Grind
item.description:
Rethinking Your Business Career in the Age of Artificial Intelligence and Robotics

AI can now do the tasks that used to define professional competence. So what’s left for humans? Everything that matters.

After the Grind is a book about the future of professional work. It argues that the repetitive cognitive labor that filled most business careers (the searching, formatting, analyzing, reporting) was never the real value. It was the training ground. AI has collapsed that training ground, and what remains is the work that machines cannot do: interpretation, integration, human connection, and imagination.

item.title: Subscribe
item.description:
Get After the Grind essays in your inbox

New essays on AI, education, and the future of human work — delivered when they publish. No spam, no fluff, unsubscribe anytime.

Powered by Buttondown. Your email is never shared.
